summary: The notebook implements an optimized inference pipeline for detecting bacterial flagellar motors in 3D tomograms. It loads a pre-trained YOLOv10 model and processes image slices with test-time augmentation and weighted box fusion to refine 2D bounding boxes. Detections are aggregated across slices using a custom 3D distance-based non-maximum suppression to identify the most confident motor location per tomogram. The pipeline prioritizes inference speed and memory efficiency through dynamic batch sizing, CUDA stream parallelization, and FP16 half-precision execution. Readers should understand how to adapt 2D object detectors for volumetric data and optimize GPU utilization for large-scale submission generation.
competition_flow: Loads test tomogram slices, runs YOLOv10 inference with TTA and WBF on each slice, merges 2D detections into 3D coordinates via custom 3D NMS, and outputs a CSV submission with the top motor detection per tomogram.
data_reading: Reads test slices as .jpg files from the competition test directory using cv2.imread with a PIL fallback.
data_processing: Weighted Box Fusion (WBF) for overlapping boxes
Dynamic batch sizing based on available GPU memory
Custom 3D distance-based non-maximum suppression to merge detections across slices
ensembling: Combines predictions from test-time augmentations (original and horizontal flip) using Weighted Box Fusion (WBF) with a confidence-weighted average, followed by a final 3D NMS to merge detections across slices.
insights: TTA combined with WBF effectively reduces false positives and refines bounding box localization for 3D motor detection.
Dynamic batch sizing and CUDA stream parallelization significantly improve inference throughput on constrained GPU memory.
Processing slices independently and merging via 3D NMS is a viable strategy for volumetric object detection without full 3D models.
